title: Critics Loved These Franchise Reboots. The Fans? Not So Much.
dek: Five requels and reboots that walked away with genuinely strong reviews — and walked straight into a wall of fan rage anyway.

items.heading: Ghostbusters (2016) Got Solid Reviews. The Internet Decided It Didn't Matter.
items.text: Critics actually liked this one — a 74% Tomatometer score with plenty of praise for its cast — but the audience score cratered to roughly 49%, and that gap didn't come out of nowhere. The all-female reboot became a target months before release, and the backlash was less about the finished film than about who was in it.

images.caption: Critics gave it a 74%. Fans gave it a much colder reception.
One of the leads praised even by critics who were lukewarm on the film overall.

items.heading: Star Wars: The Last Jedi Has One Of The Biggest Critic/Fan Splits Ever Recorded
items.text: A 93% critic score against a 54% audience score isn't a small gap — it's one of the widest ever tracked on Rotten Tomatoes for a major release. Director Rian Johnson later said he understood why: he took the story somewhere fans didn't ask for, and passionate love doesn't always survive a plot twist you didn't see coming.

images.caption: 93% with critics, 54% with the fandom that raised it.
The director who ended up defending the divide in real time.

items.heading: Terminator: Dark Fate Got Some Of The Franchise's Best Reviews In Years. It Still Bombed.
items.text: Dark Fate landed a 67% Tomatometer score, genuinely competitive for a franchise that had spent over a decade putting out critical disasters. But fans had already checked out after Genisys, and killing off a legacy character in the opening minutes only deepened the resentment, dragging the movie to one of the worst opening weekends in series history.

images.caption: Back as Sarah Connor, in a movie critics liked more than longtime fans did.

items.heading: The Matrix Resurrections Split Even Its Own Audience Score In Half
items.text: This one is genuinely strange: Resurrections' verified-audience score landed right in line with critics, but its regular, unverified audience score dropped dramatically lower — a gap analysts flagged as unusually harsh for a franchise re-up. The marketing sold a full-throttle action epic; the movie delivered a meta, humor-heavy meditation on why it exists at all, and plenty of longtime fans felt bait-and-switched.

images.caption: A rare case where the trailer promised one movie and the film delivered another.
Back as Neo, in a sequel critics found more interesting than casual fans did.

items.heading: Mulan (2020) Was Certified Fresh. Fans Wanted Their Songs Back.
items.text: Disney's live-action Mulan hit a certified-fresh 79% with critics, who mostly bought into its more serious, war-epic tone. Audiences dropped it over 20 points lower, and a lot of that frustration traced straight back to a specific creative call: stripping out the musical numbers and Mushu left longtime fans feeling like the heart of the story went with them.

images.caption: Critics called it a visual marvel. Fans missed the songs.
The lead of a remake critics rated well above its own audience score.
